reasoning: The 1-year Treasury bill rate (DTB1YR) typically reflects market expectations for the average Fed Funds rate over the upcoming 12 months. The latest available value as of late March (March 28) is ~3.88%. In the short term (April 6, April 29), 3.88% sits near the bottom of its recent 2-week range (3.83%–3.95%), making a bounce back slightly above 3.88% more likely than further decline, especially heading into potentially volatile data releases like the April 4 jobs report. However, for medium and longer horizons (June, Sept, and Mar 2026), the prevailing macroeconomic trend dominates. The recent March 2025 FOMC confirmed forward guidance projecting two 25-bps rate cuts over the remainder of 2025, and two more in 2026 towards a neutral rate near 3%. As the 12-month forward window rolls ahead, dropping current higher spot rates (4.25%-4.50% range) and rolling into lower projected rates, the 1-year yield structurally trends downwards. By late 2025 and early 2026, yields should steadily reflect an average rate below 3.88%, barring a resurgence in inflation. Conforming to realistic market dynamics, the probability of exceeding the March 30 baseline steadily decreases over longer horizons.
